feedback.text: Companies try to push this false impression that consumers are unreasonably demanding eternal support for the digital content. Or to legalize piracy. Which is dishonest and completely false narrative. No. Consumers of digital content don't demand eternal online support in a sense of maintenance such as updates - only a perpetually guaranteed access to content they paid for. Whether they paid for physical or digital copies (worth to mention that sometimes physical copy is also made unusable after a while), it shouldn't be a difference. In practice, if company is deadset in abandoning the game or service in general, there should be legal and easy way to declare software to be 'abandonware'. Not freeware, since it wasn't originally created as such, and companies will still retain copyrights included. But if it's unwilling to keep paying customers from access to thing they paid for, it's fraud at best and outright theft at worst, so they should lose their ownership rights for it, since they are obviously not willing to keep their own product. This should be enough of a deterrent for companies to voluntarily keep database access to customers who once paid for game, even if they are no longer selling it, even if servers for multiplayer are dead, even if no updates are being made, ever, even if's been 30 years already. If company is bought by another company, it must keep this legal obligation as well. If company simply dissapears, goes bankrupt, then it's original content would be automatically 'abandonware' as well.
